speaker: interviewer
text: Strawczynski nee [INAUDIBLE]. Hanna, we were talking about-- what I wanted to ask you about Czestochowa. It is such a special Polish city. And I'd like you to give us a little bit of background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Czestochowa-- Czestochowa's greatest claim to fame is in the cloister of [POLISH], meaning luminous mountain, something like that. It's a very old cloister, dating I believe from 13th or 14th century. And they have a very famous icon there, the Virgin Mary with the child. It's an interesting icon, because the Virgin Mary is black in this icon. And it's called [POLISH], or black Madonna. This is-- it's a very rich icon too. It's studded with gems. I had a babysitter, sort of my mother's helper, who would come two, three times a week to help my mother in housework.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was [? Hela, ?] Helen, [? Hela. ?] [? Hela ?] was young when I remember her. And [? Hela ?] would sometimes take care of me, go out with me. And she would take me to Czestochowa to the cloisters. So I saw the famous icon. It was very, very common for Polish nannies to take Jewish children to churches. They didn't mean to convert us in any way, just-- I loved [? Hela. ?] She was an interesting person. She was a very simple girl. And she was very attached to us. This, of course, did not disturb her, occasionally, to take part in some anti-Semitic little demonstrations, at least our neighbors told us a few times that they saw her together with her friend boyfriend, who was, unfortunately, an alcoholic, and he ruined her. However, [? Hela ?] was truly attached to us. And after the war, [? Hela ?] found me. I was then in Czestochowa. And she would drop in into this room that I shared with a few other girls. She would pick up my dirty clothes, and wash it. She did it many times when I was-- I didn't meet her during that time. I didn't pay her for it. I was busy. I was at school. And this was at that time, she was a ruined woman. And she probably died very, very soon-- alcohol, poverty, beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that she and her boyfriend took part in anti-Semitic demonstrations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about that in pre-war Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Well, this was 1930s that I'm talking about. And the wave of anti-Semitism was growing in the 1930s.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think it was a result of the rise of Hitler? Would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of it was the result of the rise of Hitler. It was already after [POLISH] died. And [POLISH] was the marshal, the creator of the Polish legions who was a socialist in his youth, actually. And actually, he apparently was a-- believed in a multinational Poland, the Ukrainians. There were many, many minorities in Poland. But the government became more and more rightist. Goering was hunting in our great forests with some of his friends from the Polish government. And there was, I remember particularly, this one organization [? ONR, ?] which meant the camp of national [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] something or other. I don't remember. They had a local, just on the opposite side of our street. And they had a little newspaper. It was called ABC. But it sounded [POLISH] in Poland. And always before this particular local, I remember there was this man who was lame. And I was afraid of him. He was selling this paper. And he was saying this little, while sending the paper, repeating this little detail-- [POLISH], which means the ABC is fighting. The ABC is vigilant. The ABC removes Jews from Poland. And so I remember this ever since. You know, I think about this street. I see this little man. Of course, yeah, there were incidents of not really pogroms, but some breaking of the windows by hooligans, more and more of it as 1939 approached.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get to 1939 a little later. Could you tell us about the Jewish community in Czestochowa, the size, the institutions, the schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course I don't know very much. But I know that it was a fairly large and thriving Jewish community, about one third of the population in Czestochowa at 120,000 people before the war. Now, it's double the number. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying one third were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: One third, about 30,000, were Jews. That's right, Jews from every possible class. And there were people in trade. A lot of Czestochowa was an industrial city. Even before the war, there was a steel mill. There were some trade schools, so trade and crafts.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish trade school, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Jewish trade school. Yeah, there was a Jewish trade school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and, of course, many workers, and many poor people, and some houses, and the usual makeup of a Jewish community. But, certainly, it wasn't the shtetl type of a population. It was much more advanced. And, certainly, socialistic ideas were very developed among Jewish people in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about Zionism as a movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: How about-- sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Zionism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes. I know a little about it, because we didn't have any connection with Zionism. But all parties strived. There were the right Zionists, the right to their left, [POLISH] and others. There was a very strong Bund group, Jewish socialists, that wanted to build their socialism and their Jewish life in Poland, as opposed to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that your parents mixed-- in a mixed milieu, or were there many Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Very much so, yeah. Mixed, you mean from the point of view of Jewish non-Jewish? Yes, yes, but the majority were-- most of them were Jewish. Mixed--

speaker: interviewer
text: More intellectuals, though, sort of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mixed politically also. We were many friends from Bund. We had many friends from Bund. And the generation of my parents was absolutely fascinating. I think about them often. They were-- they were just out of the ghettos, this generation. They broke out of the ghetto. They broke with the-- with the very religious and very traditional part of the society. And they were very often self-educated, but amazingly educated. My father was an example. But I met some other people like that. They were people who were dreaming. They had a dream. Like King had a dream, they had a dream, too, of equality, of justice, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] racial equality, of course, a world with no anti-Semitism. And this is what brought many of them to the Communist party. It's terribly sad that most of them, if they did not perish during the war murdered by Hitler, they perished in Soviet camps, murdered by Stalin.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is ironic.

speaker: interviewee
text: This God that failed, yeah. But they were true idealists. It's hard to-- to find people like that now.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can see that you grew up with a very stimulating atmosphere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did mention to me that you wanted to tell us about one of your neighbors, a [? Hirsch ?] [? Fryman. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk-- end off with the pre-war period.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, maybe I'll mention him when I talk about the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: We can go to that now, in 1939, how things deteriorated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, 1939, of course, was a very tense-- the end of 1939, like September, or the end of the summer, there was a lot of talk about the war. We, of course, you know, listened to what was going on in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a radio at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we did. Yeah, we did have a radio at home because we listened to music on the radio too, yeah. And I remember very clearly the few days before the outbreak of the war, and then this Friday of September, 1939. This was the first of September, 1939, Friday. What was happening was that my brother, who was then in the last few grades of high school, had as a subject at school military training. This was a compulsory subject in high schools for boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had little uniforms. And they were taught target shooting. They had rifles, this type of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the beginning of the war on the first, they were called in to school as a group. And we, the two of us, were left at home. My father was in Warsaw, of course. The panic was incredible. People were terribly afraid of gas, poison gas. They remembered it from the First World War. They were afraid of bombs. And my brave mother, who was always so cool and brave, lost her head completely. There was a group of neighbors, a large group of neighbors, going to escape running. And she joined them with me, leaving [POLISH], my brother, there. We started to run.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went on the road. The road was-- this was the first horror that I saw. This was a real mayhem, what was going on in the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were thousands and thousands of people going all directions-- people from the cities who are escaping to the country. People from the country were escaping to the cities. There were some military units. There were wagons, horses. And there were German planes. And these small planes, I believe they were called [POLISH], they would dive down so we could see, actually, the pilot. And they would machine gun people, civilian population. And I remember many attacks like this, lying in the fields. And I don't know how we survived this. This is the first sight of death and dying that I had in my life. There were a lot of wounded, and dying, and dead people and horses lying around. Anyhow, this didn't last long, because our invaders caught up with us very quickly. And we returned to Czestochowa after four or five days. My mother was going crazy about what happened to [POLISH]. But we found him safe at home. They never took any part in any fight. Czestochowa did not fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came back. And we came back to here, where it happened in Czestochowa on Sunday, which means on the third day of the war we were still on the road. On this-- no, it was Monday. They came in on Sunday, the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on Monday, they rounded up hundreds and hundreds of people, Jews and Poles. They, at that time-- at that moment, they didn't make any difference. They marched them through the city, beating them, and hitting them, and ridiculing them. And they machine gunned a lot of them. I only heard it. I didn't see any of that. But this day was called the Bloody Monday in Czestochowa. So this was the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the immediate effect on your family and the Jewish community of the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Jews right from the beginning started to pray. And the Germans, particularly Jews in traditional grab Jews with beards, right away they started to cut the beards, to ask these Jews to jump in the streets like dogs, like rabbits, like groups of soldiers just looking at it, and laughing their heads off, and making pictures. This started right away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But, of course, nobody, nobody, could ever imagine what's going to happen. I mean, our imagination did not go that far. So Jews like Jews-- you know, Jews were used to different times. And, well, we'll survive this too. We have to be careful. We have to, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, anyhow, I didn't stay long in Czestochowa. Were their restrictions that came into effect before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, right away, there were restrictions. There was a curfew. And there was-- I don't believe that the arm bands in Czestochowa came that early, no. Oh, we had to give up our radios, money of course, gems, all types of constant ransom type of situation. And the [POLISH] was formed in Czestochowa quite early. But I don't remember this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were going to leave? You left with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my brother, and myself left Czestochowa because we got news, finally-- I mean after it calmed down a little bit, we got news from my father that he is going to the Russian side. You remember that on the 17th of September, 1939, the Russians came in. And Poland was divided. They took the part of-- the eastern part of Poland. And there was a border now between the Russians and the Germans. And these two were allies after the MolotovRibbentrop Pact. So my father escaped to the Russian side. And he told us in his message to come to Warsaw. And from Warsaw, he will smuggle us to be with him. So my mother packed up whatever we had. She sold these few pieces of furniture that we had, closed the place. We spent the last few days with this neighbor that I mentioned before, [? Mr. Fryman ?] and his family. This was a wonderful family. He was a tin smith. There was always the faint smell of tar around him, because he worked on the roofs very often. And we were very, very friendly. I'll talk about him later on, about what happened to him in the ghetto, in the small ghetto. We spent a few day-- the few last days in Czestochowa with this family. And we went by train to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it risky for you to travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, it was just at that time as risky as for everyone. It was very crowded. And, of course, there were round-- round ups constantly. They were catching people, both Poles, Jews, whoever, for hard labor, for dirty labor, to send to Germany. You have to be careful. But the trains started to go. And they were very, very crowded. But we did get on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Czestochowa from Warsaw, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm afraid I can't tell you in the number of kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite a few hours on train. And now in Czes-- in Warsaw, we came to Warsaw in the late afternoon, but still before the curfew. And we knew Warsaw from before the war. It was a nice, very lively city, a bit city. It looked awful. There were ruins. Warsaw was fighting. Warsaw was-- three weeks, I believe was siege of Warsaw. And it was bombed heavily. So we saw the ruins, rubble on the street. It was late October, I believe, or maybe early November, because there snow already And it was very, very cold. And we went into the Jewish quarter, I mean the traditional Jewish quarter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What streets would those have been? Would you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'll be talking about the street maybe later on. But what we were going to do was we were going to stay with a woman and a child in the same situation that we were. They were friends of my father. Her husband was also there. And she was also supposed to-- so it was a communal apartment with four rooms. And each family lived in a different room with a common kitchen. We were going to live with this woman. The moment we came into this Jewish quarter-- it was not yet the official ghetto, but this is where the ghetto later on happened-- I was quite frightened. Parts of it were already barbed wired. And there were these big signs-- sign board, no billboards-- epidemics of typhus, beware, keep away, this type of things. It was a very dirty, very empty, in the streets, many windows taped and covered with paper. And it was-- it was quite frightening. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stayed in this apartment. And within two weeks, my brother took off. My father sent some people to smuggle him through the border. The border at that time was sometimes open, sometimes closed. But it was becoming closed. And he joined my father in Bialystok. I talk about what happened to them later. Soon afterwards, the border was closed between the Russian part and the German part of Poland. People were still going. But it was not very safe. And at that point, my father decided that he will get us a visa to come in officially, not to be submitted to the danger of crossing this illegal border. This, of course, was a tragic mistake. And this is how we were stuck with my mother in the Warsaw ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You asked me about the street. We lived on [? Jelna ?] Street, [? Jelna ?] 17. [? Jelna ?] means brave. And you had to be brave to live there. We lived on the fourth floor, front room. We were facing a big and famous Warsaw prison, the [POLISH]. From our-- from our window, we could see the [POLISH], the courtyard. We could see the sentry boxes, and the guards walking around. But they could see us too. And they watched for us. And if they saw anybody in the window, they would shoot. And sometimes, they were just shooting to amuse themselves. So our windows were permanently covered with black paper. And I was strongly, strongly admonished by my mother not to go close to the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a lot of fear living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a lot of fear, yeah. I was 12 then. I turned 12 in October. So I was really very young. And there's a lot of things I don't remember. But since I was young, my mother still sheltered me as much as she could. So there's a lot of things I was not at the beginning exposed to. We didn't have money. To go and get some learning, to draw in some illegal teaching courses, classes, we didn't have the money. We didn't really know a lot of people in Warsaw. We were together with this lady. And there was very little I could do. Going out was not safe. I'm talking about the first few months. So it's very, very early. The ghetto was not officially there, not yet. And we were wearing arm bands.
